role_summary: Saint Augustine, Florida native; witness as a child to Ku Klux Klan marches through the Lincolnville Black neighborhood; one of the African American students who integrated previously all-white St. Augustine public schools in the late 1960s; later an organizer of the 40th ACCORD project commemorating the St. Augustine civil rights movement.
ai_reading: Duncan's four nearest neighbors by shared-passage count are the tightest St. Augustine grouping in the catalog: Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (entry 103, count 94), Purcell Maurice Conway (entry 99, count 90), Barbara Edna Vickers (entry 5, count 90), and the joint Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer interview (entry 4, count 78), with C. T. Vivian (entry 134, count 64) fifth. The cosine cluster reconstructs the Lincolnville cohort itself: Hayling advised the NAACP Youth Council and was part of the organizing that drew Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine in 1964, Vickers and Conway were Lincolnville neighbors inside that same campaign, and Duncan was a child during the arc and integrated the schools that followed it. Two concept-axis readings cut against the surface of a child-witness oral history. On the Nonviolence as Theology to Armed Self-Defense axis the catalog places her at +0.89, the third-deepest Nonviolence as Theology reading in the catalog, even though her own schoolhouse memory pulls the other way: she describes the forced school integration as a setting where "it wasn't non-violence," where students "had to fight even to eat." The embedding reads the surrounding transcript through the nonviolent vocabulary of the St. Augustine cohort and the 1964 King campaign rather than through that one remembered fight, a placement her firsthand account complicates. On the Tactical Pragmatism to Strategic Vision axis she sits at -0.89, the second-deepest Strategic Vision reading in the catalog, which also runs against the surface: the transcript is built from concrete lived detail (the colored sign at the doctor's office, the hallway she was forbidden to walk, the markers and family-reunion logistics of the ACCORD commemoration), yet the model places that detail near the long-horizon pole rather than the ground-level one.
biographical_paragraph: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan was born June 28, 1956 and raised in the historically Black Lincolnville neighborhood of Saint Augustine, Florida, where her oral history situates her as a fifth-generation native descended from the Finley family that settled in St. Johns County [src: 1]. Her interview for the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) is held jointly by the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture as entry 51 of the collection [src: 1, src: 2]. She recounts witnessing, at about seven, Ku Klux Klan marches through Lincolnville during the period that drew Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine, the same segregationist mobilization that the local NAACP Youth Council adviser Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (catalog entry 103) and the Lincolnville organizer Barbara Edna Vickers (catalog entry 5) were confronting through 1963 and 1964 [src: 1, src: 3]. She describes entering a forced school-integration cohort in the sixth grade and graduating through St. Augustine High School [src: 1]. As an adult she helped found and lead the 40th ACCORD commemoration project, organizing the recognition of the city's 1963-1964 movement and the placement of historic markers across St. Augustine [src: 1, src: 7]; fellow St. Augustine interviewees Purcell Maurice Conway (entry 99) and Audrey Nell Hamilton (entry 4) both credit her by name with that commemoration work in their own oral histories. Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked to begin with the movement, Duncan named the memory that came first from her childhood in Lincolnville:
interview_snippets.quote: What I remember most from a child at seven and a half years old when Dr. King came to St. Augustine was the marches by the Ku Klux Klan.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Walking home after Black residents lined the streets to watch a nighttime Klan march pass through the neighborhood:
interview_snippets.quote: And I can remember, as we were walking home, my mom holding my sister's hands in mine, and she said, don't look back.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
interview_snippets.lead_in: On entering the forced school-integration cohort in the sixth grade after her elementary school closed:
interview_snippets.quote: But I can remember going to be in force to go to the schools and we had to fight. It looked like it was a non-violence. We had to fight even to eat, you know.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling a planning meeting where one attendee asked why the commemoration organizers would not let the past rest, and movement veteran J. T. Johnson answered:
interview_snippets.quote: And J.T. Johnson stood up and he said, it's just time to just say thank you.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
interview_snippets.lead_in: On why the recorded record of St. Augustine's movement mattered to her:
interview_snippets.quote: So if anybody watches this 50, 60, 70 years from today, they'll know that there was some great people that walked among us, angels I call them.

interview_snippets.speaker: Purcell Maurice Conway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Fellow Lincolnville native Purcell Maurice Conway credits Duncan and her family with launching the ACCORD commemoration:
interview_snippets.quote: I mean, they Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan and her family took this task on by themselves some years ago in the old three.

interview_snippets.speaker: Audrey Nell Hamilton
interview_snippets.lead_in: St. Augustine demonstrator Audrey Nell Hamilton recalls directing Duncan to Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (entry 103) as she began the commemoration research:
interview_snippets.quote: She called me and I told her, I said, Gwendolyn, if you want to know anything or need to know anything, you need to get in touch with Dr. Robert B. Hayling.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Bagner Hayling
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling, recorded the day after Duncan, refers back to her testimony about her family physician in the same St. Augustine interview series:
interview_snippets.quote: Mrs. (Gwendolyn) Duncan mentioned that Mayor Shelley who was a practicing physician was her family physician and all.

movement_context: Duncan's childhood in 1960s Lincolnville sits inside the most concentrated documented period of civil rights direct action in northeast Florida. Lincolnville, founded by formerly enslaved residents in 1866 immediately south of the colonial-era city core, was both the residential base for St. Augustine's Black population and the organizing infrastructure node out of which the early-1960s campaign ran: Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling's dental practice on Bridge Street and the local NAACP Youth Council meeting spaces sat within walking distance of Duncan's childhood streets [src: 3]. The Klan parade Duncan witnessed as a child belongs to the documentary record of segregationist counter-mobilization that Hayling and his NAACP Youth Council were responding to through 1963 and 1964, including the September 18, 1963 Klan rally at which Hayling, James Hauser, Clyde Jenkins, and James Jackson were captured, beaten, and nearly killed before sheriff's deputies intervened [src: 3, 4]. The school integration cohort Duncan joined in the late 1960s implemented the desegregation order issued in the St. Johns County school district after the Supreme Court's 1968 Green v. County School Board of New Kent County ruling closed the freedom-of-choice loophole that southern districts had used through the mid-1960s to delay compliance with Brown v. Board of Education [src: 5]. The St. Augustine implementation, like the parallel implementations across rural and small-city Florida, placed small numbers of Black students into majority-white school buildings without the structural supports of bused-in cohort size or local civil rights protection, and oral histories from the Florida desegregation generation consistently document the social-isolation and physical-intimidation costs the integrating students absorbed across the late 1960s and early 1970s [src: 5, 6]. Duncan's testimony places her inside that documented cohort, with the additional regional specificity that her schoolhouse desegregation occurred in the same city whose preceding direct-action campaign had drawn Martin Luther King Jr. to its bayfront just four summers earlier [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: Duncan's later participation in the ACCORD Freedom Trail (the St. Augustine commemoration project founded in 2004 by local civil rights veterans to mark the sites of the 1963-1964 organizing campaign) places her oral history alongside the documentary record the project itself assembles [src: 7]. The ACCORD Civil Rights Museum operates from the Rudcarlie Building on Bridge Street in Lincolnville, within the same residential geography Duncan grew up in, and the Freedom Trail's site markers across the city annotate the public locations where the 1964 demonstrations, kneel-ins, and beach wade-ins occurred [src: 7]. In her oral history Duncan recounts that the Northrop Grumman corporation funded thirty historic civil rights markers in and around the city after state, county, and federal funding requests had gone unapproved [src: 1]. Her oral history was recorded for the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) and is held at the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture as joint stewards of the collection [src: 1, src: 2].

gallery.caption: St. Augustine civil rights demonstrations, 1964, from the Florida Memory project of the State Library and Archives of Florida. The footage documents the spring 1964 SCLC direct-action campaign in St. Augustine, the organizing arc Duncan witnessed as a child in Lincolnville and whose outcome included the school-desegregation order she helped implement four years later.
gallery.photographer: State Library and Archives of Florida (Florida Memory project)

gallery.alt: Lincolnville Historic District historical marker, St. Augustine, Florida
gallery.caption: Historical marker for the Lincolnville Historic District in St. Augustine, the historically Black neighborhood founded in 1866 by formerly enslaved residents where Duncan grew up and witnessed the Ku Klux Klan parade described in her oral history. Lincolnville served as the residential and organizing-infrastructure base for the 1963-1964 St. Augustine civil rights campaign.

gallery.alt: ACCORD Civil Rights Museum in the Rudcarlie Building, Bridge Street, Lincolnville, St. Augustine, Florida
gallery.caption: The ACCORD Civil Rights Museum, housed in the Rudcarlie Building on Bridge Street in the Lincolnville neighborhood. The museum is operated by the ACCORD Freedom Trail commemoration project, which Duncan participated in and which documents and marks the sites of the 1963-1964 St. Augustine civil rights organizing campaign.
